“慢点，你走得太快了，…”--保罗·西蒙(Paul Simon)1966年的“第59街大桥之歌”(The第59 Street Bridge Song，Feelin‘Groovy)。
我们崇尚效率。少用多得。当天送货。多任务；在一台设备上发送文本，同时在另一台设备上收发电子邮件，可能还会在第三台设备上交谈。效率被认为是好的。低效是浪费。
这样想是有充分理由的。经济学家告诉我们，提高效率是提高我们生活水平的主要途径。如果你的公司给你加薪但没有变得更有效率，它也将不得不提高价格来弥补缺口。如果所有的公司都这么做，每个人最终都会原地踏步-你需要更高的工资来匹配你购买的商品的更高价格。所以，如果我们想要取得物质上的进步，我们必须变得更有效率。精简的供应链，准时送货，劳动力不松懈，都有助于提高效率。做到这一点，我们所有的生活都会变得越来越好，或者说，我们得到了这样的承诺。
对于汽车制造商来说，他们希望从汽车设计中挤出尽可能多的每加仑里程，空气阻力和抢占道路是效率的敌人。在金融界，大多数摩擦都是在交汇点上产生的。在赚钱之前，土豆农场主不得不用一袋袋土豆来换取鸡蛋和牛奶。正如英国历史学家尼尔·弗格森(Niall Ferguson)在他的著作“货币的上升”(The Ascent of Money，2008)中提醒我们的那样，货币的发明在很大程度上降低了这种低效率，过去200年来金融界发生的许多事情都可以视为这场革命的延续。
例如，信用意味着即使现在没有钱，你也可以去买鸡蛋和牛奶。自那以后，金融市场将这种效率提升到了另一个水平。“期权市场”的建立意味着你不必费心去买一只你很快就要卖出的股票。您只需承诺购买，然后按期权合同规定的价格和日期出售即可。然后你可以交易期权，而不是标的股票。
这些发展中的每一项，以及其他许多发展，都使做生意变得更容易，不浪费时间和精力，也不会产生摩擦。每一次都使经济交易更快、更有效率。从某些方面来说，这显然是件好事。但2008年的金融危机表明，可能会有太多的好事。如果抵押贷款和其他贷款没有转化为可交易资产(“证券”)，那么银行家们可能会花时间评估每个申请者的信用状况。如果人们不得不去银行取款，他们可能会减少消费，增加储蓄。这不仅仅是猜测--例如，诺贝尔奖获得者经济学家理查德·泰勒(Richard Thaler)回顾的一项研究显示，人们用信用卡购买商品的价格比现金高。可以说，一点摩擦就能让我们放慢脚步，使机构和个人都能做出更好的财务决策。
十年前，美国心理学家亚当·格兰特(Adam Grant)和我在一篇期刊论文中辩称，这种“太好的东西”现象可能是一个普遍规律。有些动机会带来出色的表现，过多的动机会让人窒息。有些团队合作会产生凝聚力，提高生产力；过多的团队合作会导致陈旧。一些同理心可以让你了解别人正在经历的事情；太多的同理心可能会阻止你说出和做一些艰难的事情。同样，在我的书“选择的悖论”(The Paradox of Choice，2004)中，我认为，没有自由选择的生活不值得过，而选择太多的生活会导致瘫痪、糟糕的决定和不满。无论是作为个人还是作为一个社会，找到激励、合作、同理心、选择和生活的许多其他方面(包括效率)的适当量-亚里士多德所说的“平均数”-是我们面临的一个关键挑战。
但要找到中庸之道并非易事。正如英国诗人威廉·布莱克(William Blake)在“天堂与地狱的婚姻”(The Marry of Heaven and Hell，1790-93)中所说：“除非你知道什么是足够的，否则你永远不知道什么是足够的。”
如果金融危机告诉我们，我们的交易变得太有效率了，那么新冠肺炎大流行又是怎么回事呢？为什么我们没有储备关键的补给和机器，建立医院的能力，或者确保我们供应链的健壮性？当然，原因是这会被认为是低效和掠夺利润的行为。花在仓库里积灰的口罩和长袍上的钱总是可以在市场上更“高效”地使用。同样，雇佣比“普通”情况下需要更多的人，或者自己制造产品而不是依赖国际供应链，都会被视为效率低下。那么，其中一个教训是，为了下一次做好更好的准备，我们需要学会在此时此地“效率”较低的生活。
从这个角度来看，至少有一些低效就像是一份保险单。想想你自己的情况。每年，如果你没有发生车祸，房子没有烧毁，身体健康，你可能会对自己说，你把钱‘浪费’在各种毫无意义的保险产品上了，如果没有那些保险费，你的经济状况会更好。
我们大多数人都不喜欢把钱浪费在保险上的感觉。我们宁愿穿着那些钱，或者吃它，或者开它。几年前，经过一番努力，我说服了年迈的母亲用一种更全面的保险产品来补充她的基本健康保险。她的资源有限，政策也不便宜。一年过去了，令人高兴的是，她没有严重的身体状况需要使用额外的保险。当续签的时间到了，我母亲拒绝了，因为事实上，她前一年花的钱已经“浪费”了。我的回答也许有点尖酸刻薄，我的回答是向她建议，也许明年她会很幸运，得了一场真正严重的疾病，然后从她的保险中拿出她的钱的价值。
值得庆幸的是，在许多领域，政府法规通过强迫我们购买保险来保护我们，使我们不再渴望更高的个人财务效率。法律要求我们的汽车投保，抵押者也要求我们的房屋投保。在美国，“奥巴马医改”(2010年颁布的“平价医疗法案”(Affordable Care Act)旨在增加医疗保险覆盖的美国公民人数)实质上是强迫人们购买医疗保险，直到最高法院质疑该法案的这一方面违宪。我怀疑我们中的许多人总体上保险不足，但如果没有这些各种各样的、国家强制实施的保险要求，问题会严重得多。
思考保险的一种方式，无论它可能看起来多么低效，都是使我们能够弹性地抵御来自一个根本不确定的世界可能降临到我们身上的冲击。这个世界从根本上说是不确定的。正如英国经济学家约翰·凯(John Kay)和默文·金(Mervyn King)在他们的书“激进的不确定性(2020)”中指出的那样，通过将概率附加到各种不太可能的未来世界状态来量化风险的努力大多是科幻小说。这个世界比轮盘赌盘或一对骰子杂乱得多。
面对这种根本性的不确定性，我们应该做些什么呢？在做决定时，我们不应该问自己哪种选择会给我们带来最好的结果，而应该问哪种选择会给我们带来好的结果-在世界上最广泛的未来状态下，哪种选择会给我们足够的结果。与其试图最大化我们退休账户的投资回报，我们应该设定一个财务目标，然后选择能够让我们在未来最广泛的财务状况下实现这一目标的投资。我们不应该寻找“最好的”工作，而应该寻找一份足够好、足够令人满意的工作，因为同事和经理们来来去去，未来的经济也在摇摆。我们不应该选择最好的大学去，而应该选择一所足够好的大学，即使有一个令人讨厌的室友和一个乏味的生物1老师。
用来描述这种决策方法的术语是令人满意的。而着眼于根本不确定的未来的满足感可能被称为稳健满足感。满足是一种保险形式--针对金融危机、全球流行病、令人讨厌的老板、乏味的教师和糟糕的室友提供保险。保险可能看起来很古板-就像那个系着腰带和吊带的人。也许我们两个都不需要，但是如果我们两个都不需要会发生什么呢？
我认为，2008年金融危机暴露出的资本主义的真正缺陷是它对利润和效率的无节制、一心一意的追求。也许我们对2019-20年大流行缺乏准备所揭示的真正缺陷就是同样的事情的表现。资本主义不必肆无忌惮，也不必固执己见。这在其他生活水平较高的社会中是不存在的，在美国历史上也没有出现过。因此，也许现在是时候重新点燃某些社会规范了，这些社会规范对贫民窟起到了推波助澜的作用。
我们都想要一辆每加仑能跑100英里的车。拖累我们的摩擦力是一种代价高昂的烦恼。当我们开车的时候，我们知道我们要去哪里，我们在控制之中，所以快速感觉很好，尽管即使在这里，当你遇到结冰的道路时，一点点摩擦也可以预防灾难。
开车已经够危险的了，生活并不像开车那样可以预测。我们并不总是知道我们要去哪里。我们并不总是处于控制之中。到处都是黑色的冰。在我们生活的这个不确定的世界里，一些能让我们放慢脚步的小东西可能会是我们的救命稻草。无论是作为个人还是作为一个社会，在我们的生活中建立摩擦，就是在系统中建立韧性。这可能是我们针对灾难的保险单。
note_en:
‘Slow down, you move too fast  …’  – ‘The 59th Street Bridge Song (Feelin’ Groovy)’ (1966) by Paul Simon
 We worship efficiency. Use less to get more. Same-day delivery. Multitask; text on one device while emailing on a second, and perhaps conversing on a third. Efficiency is seen as good. Inefficiency as wasteful.
 There’s a sound rationale for thinking this way. Economists teach us that increased efficiency is the major way to improve our standard of living. If your company gives you a pay rise without becoming more efficient, it will also have to raise its prices to make up the shortfall. If all companies do the same, everyone ends up running in place – you’ll need your higher wages to match the higher prices of the things you buy. So, if we want to make material progress, we must become more efficient. Streamlined supply chains, just-in-time deliveries and no slack in the workforce all serve to raise efficiency. Achieve this, and all our lives will get better and better, or so we’re promised.
 For automobile manufacturers, who wish to squeeze as many miles per gallon as possible out of their car designs, air resistance and the grab of the road are the enemies of efficiency. In the world of finance, it is at the point of exchange that most friction arises. Before money, the potato farmer had to use sacks of potatoes to trade for eggs and milk. As the British historian Niall Ferguson reminds us in his  book  The Ascent of Money (2008), the invention of money went a long way toward reducing this inefficiency, and much that has happened in the financial world over the past 200 years can be seen as a continuation of that revolution.
 Credit, for example, meant that you could go shopping for eggs and milk even without having the money right now. Financial markets have since taken this efficiency to another level. The creation of ‘option markets’ means that you don’t have to go to the trouble of buying a stock that you’re going to be selling soon anyway. You can just promise to buy it, and then sell it at a price and date specified by the option contract. And then you can trade the option rather than the underlying stock.
 Each of these developments and many others have made it easier to do one’s business without wasted time and energy – without friction. Each has made economic transactions quicker and more efficient. That’s obviously good in some ways. But the financial crisis of 2008 suggested that maybe there could be too much of a good thing. If mortgages and other loans hadn’t been transformed into tradable assets (‘securities’), then bankers might have taken the time to assess the credit-worthiness of each applicant. If people had to visit a bank to withdraw cash, they might spend less and save more. This is not mere speculation – for instance,  research  reviewed by the Nobel Prize-winning economist Richard Thaler shows that people will pay more for an item with a credit card than with cash. Arguably, a little friction to slow us down would have enabled both institutions and individuals to make better financial decisions.
 A decade ago, the American psychologist Adam Grant and I argued in a journal  paper that this ‘too much of a good thing’ phenomenon might be a general rule. Some motivation produces excellent performance; too much motivation produces choking. Some group collaboration produces cohesion and enhances productivity; too much of it leads to staleness. Some empathy enables you to understand what another person is going through; too much could prevent you from saying and doing hard things. Similarly, in my  book  The Paradox of Choice (2004), I argued that, whereas a life with no freedom to choose is not worth living, a life with too much choice leads to paralysis, bad decisions and dissatisfaction. Finding the right amount – what Aristotle called the ‘mean’ – of motivation, collaboration, empathy, choice and many other aspects of life, including efficiency, is a key challenge we face, both as individuals and as a society.
  But finding the mean isn’t easy. As the English poet William Blake observed in  The Marriage of Heaven and Hell (1790-93): ‘You never know what is enough unless you know what is more than enough.’
 If the financial crisis taught us that we had become too efficient with our transactions, what of the COVID-19 pandemic? Why hadn’t we stockpiled key supplies and machines, built up hospital capacity, or ensured the robustness of our supply chains? The reason, of course, is that it would have been seen as inefficient and profit-robbing. Money spent on masks and gowns gathering dust in a warehouse could always be put to more ‘productive’ use in the marketplace. Likewise, employing more people than needed under ‘ordinary’ circumstances, or making products yourself rather than relying on international supply chains, would have been seen as inefficient. One lesson, then, is that to be better prepared next time, we need to learn to live less ‘efficiently’ in the here and now.
 Seen in this light, at least some inefficiency is like an insurance policy. Think about your own situation. Every year that you don’t get into a car accident and your house doesn’t burn down and you stay healthy, you could think to yourself that you have ‘wasted’ your money on various pointless insurance products, and that you’d be financially better off without all those insurance premiums to pay.
 Most of us don’t like the sense that we’re wasting money on insurance. We would rather be wearing that money, or eating it, or driving it. Some years ago, with a struggle, I convinced my ageing mother to supplement her basic health insurance policy with a more comprehensive insurance product. Her resources were modest and the policy wasn’t cheap. The year went by and, happily, she had no serious medical conditions that required the use of the extra cover. When the time came to renew, my mother resisted, because, indeed, the money she spent the year before had been ‘wasted’. My reply, perhaps unduly snarky, was to suggest to her that maybe the next year she would get lucky, have a really serious illness, and get her money’s worth out of her insurance.
 Thankfully, in many domains, government regulations protect us from our desire for ever-greater personal financial efficiency by forcing us to have insurance. Laws require that our cars be insured, and mortgagers require the same for our homes. In the United States, ‘Obamacare’ (the Affordable Care Act enacted in 2010, designed to increase the number of US citizens covered by health insurance) essentially compelled people to have health insurance, until the Supreme Court challenged this aspect of the Act as unconstitutional. I suspect that many of us are underinsured in general, but the problem would be much worse without these various, state-imposed insurance requirements.
 One way to think about insurance, however inefficient it might feel, is that it enables us to be resilient against shocks that could befall us from a world that is radically uncertain. And the world  is radically uncertain. As the British economists John Kay and Mervyn King point out in their  book  Radical Uncertainty (2020), efforts to quantify risk by attaching probabilities to various unlikely future states of the world are mostly science fiction. The world is much messier than a roulette wheel or a pair of dice.
  What should we do in the face of this radical uncertainty? When making decisions, instead of asking ourselves which option will give us the best results, we should be asking which option will give us good-enough results under the widest range of future states of the world. Instead of trying to maximise return on investment in our retirement account, we should be setting a financial goal and then choosing investments that will allow us to achieve that goal under the widest set of future financial circumstances. Instead of looking for the ‘best’ job, we should be looking for a job that will be good enough – satisfying enough – as co-workers and managers come and go, and the future economy gyrates. Instead of choosing the best college to go to, we should be choosing a college that will be good enough, even with an obnoxious roommate and a boring Bio 1 teacher.
 The term used to describe this approach to decision-making is  satisficing. And satisficing with an eye toward a radically uncertain future might be called  robust satisficing. Satisficing is a form of insurance – insurance against financial meltdowns, global pandemics, nasty bosses, boring teachers and crappy roommates. Insurance can seem stodgy – like the guy who wears a belt  and suspenders. Perhaps we don’t need both, but what happens if we have neither?
 I think the real flaw in capitalism revealed by the 2008 financial crisis was its unbridled, single-minded pursuit of profit and efficiency. And perhaps the real flaw revealed in our lack of readiness for the 2019-20 pandemic was a manifestation of the same thing. Capitalism needn’t be either unbridled or single-minded. It isn’t in other societies with high standards of living, and it hasn’t been at all points in history in the US. So perhaps it’s time to rekindle certain social norms that serve to slow us down. For example, if people thought about their homes less as financial investments and more as places to live, full of the friction of kids, dogs, friends, neighbours and community, there might be less property speculation with an eye toward buying and selling houses merely for profit. If companies felt the friction of being caretakers of their communities, they might look differently at streamlining their operations by eliminating jobs.
 We’d all like a car that gets 100 miles to a gallon. The forces of friction that slow us down are an expensive annoyance. When we’re driving, we know where we’re going and we’re in control, so fast feels good, though even here, a little bit of friction can forestall disaster when you encounter an icy road.
 Driving is perilous enough, and life is not as predictable as driving. We don’t always know where we’re going. We’re not always in control. Black ice is everywhere. A little something to slow us down in the uncertain world we inhabit could be a life-saver. Building friction into our lives, as individuals and as a society, is building resilience into the system. It could be our insurance policy against catastrophe.
title: 为什么效率是危险的，放慢速度会让生活变得更好
title_en: Why efficiency is dangerous and slowing down makes life better

亚马逊周五在一份监管申报文件中宣布，亚马逊全球消费者部门首席执行官杰夫·威尔克(Jeff Wilke)计划于2021年第一季度退休。
威尔克负责亚马逊的几个高优先级业务线，并被认为是首席执行官杰夫·贝佐斯的可能继任者。这位土生土长的匹兹堡人(也是钢人队的粉丝)于1999年加入该公司，并帮助亚马逊将物流业务打造成今天的庞然大物。2016年，他被任命负责公司的全球消费者业务。
据华尔街日报报道，他在周五给员工的一份备忘录中表示，现在是“我花时间探索个人兴趣的时候了，这些兴趣已经退居次要地位20多年了”，并补充说，他正专注于让亚马逊为即将到来的假日购物季做好准备。
贝佐斯在给员工的一封电子邮件中说，威尔克在他不在的情况下成立了公司，是为了在他不在的情况下取得成功，他称他是一名“导师”。
贝佐斯写道：“杰夫的遗产和影响力将在他离开后长久存在。”“没有他，亚马逊就会面目全非。”
威尔克是亚马逊最近宣布离职的几位高管之一。机器人副总裁布拉德·波特(Brad Porter)上周宣布，他将离职加入一家人工智能公司。亚马逊网络服务的杰出工程师彼得·沃斯希尔于2月退休，亚马逊航空部门的保罗·维奥拉也于今年早些时候离职。
亚马逊零售运营高级副总裁戴夫·克拉克将接替威尔克。根据彭博社(Bloomberg)去年的一篇个人资料，克拉克以访问亚马逊仓库以评估人们是否足够努力而闻名，这为他赢得了“狙击手”的绰号。
note_en:
Jeff Wilke, CEO of Amazon’s worldwide consumer division, is planning to retire in the first quarter of 2021, the company  announced in a regulatory filing on Friday.
 Wilke oversees several of Amazon’s high-priority business lines and was considered a possible successor to CEO Jeff Bezos. The Pittsburgh native ( and Steelers fan) joined the company in 1999 and helped build Amazon’s logistics operations into the behemoth it is today. He was put in charge of the company’s global consumer business in 2016.
 He said in a memo to staff on Friday that it was “time for me to take time to explore personal interests that have taken a back seat for over two decades,”  according to  The Wall Street Journal, adding that he was focused on preparing Amazon for the upcoming holiday shopping season.
 Bezos  said in an email to employees that Wilke had set up the company to succeed in his absence, referring to him as a “tutor.”
 “Jeff’s legacy and impact will live on long after he departs,” Bezos wrote. “He is simply one of those people without whom Amazon would be completely unrecognizable.”
 Wilke is among several top Amazon execs who have recently announced they’re leaving the company. Vice president of robotics  Brad Porter announced last week that he is leaving to join an AI company. Peter Vosshall, a distinguished engineer on Amazon Web Services,  retired in February, and Paul Viola of Amazon’s Air division left earlier this year as well.
 Dave Clark, Amazon’s senior vice president of retail operations, will succeed Wilke.  According to a  Bloomberg profile from last year, Clark has a reputation for visiting Amazon warehouses to assess whether people were working hard enough, which earned him the nickname “The Sniper.”
title: 亚马逊第二重要的杰夫即将离开公司
title_en: The second-most important Jeff at Amazon is leaving the company

Spotify在忘记续订作为其服务一部分的证书后，今天遭遇了一次短暂的停机。
在美国东部时间今天上午8点左右，Spotify用户开始在Twitter上报告他们无法连接到该服务，它只会显示一个错误，说明无法加载页面，&#34；
根据Cloudflare网络工程师Louis Poinsignon的说法，Spotify主机名*.wg.spotify.com的通配符证书尚未续订和过期。
过期的证书将导致与这些主机通信的Spotify服务出现问题，这可能会导致中断。
一小时后，Poinsignon表示证书再次续签，与此同时，Spotify的官方支持账号在推特上表示，该服务已经恢复。
不幸的是，由于大多数服务已切换到安全连接，证书过期导致的中断和问题正变得越来越普遍。
本月早些时候，加州卡雷迪传染病报告和监测系统的一份证书到期，导致该州新冠肺炎病例漏报。
在过去，我们也看到过期的证书影响了Facebook的Tor服务器、微软团队和其他物联网设备等服务。
劳伦斯·艾布拉姆斯是BleepingComputer.com的创建者和所有者。劳伦斯的专业领域包括恶意软件删除和计算机取证。劳伦斯·艾布拉姆斯(Lawrence Abrams)是“Winterals碎片整理、恢复和管理现场指南”的合著者，也是Rootkit for Dummies的技术编辑。
note_en:
Spotify was hit with a brief outage today after they forgot to renew a certificate used as part of their service.
 At approximately 8 AM EST today, Spotify users began to report on Twitter that they were unable to connect to the service and it would just display an error stating it "Couldn't load the page,"
  According to Cloudflare network engineer Louis Poinsignon, a wildcard certificate for the Spotify hostname *.wg.spotify.com had not been renewed and expired.
 The expired certificate would cause Spotify services that communicate with these hosts to have issues, which likely led to the outage.
  An hour later, Poinsignon stated that the certificate was renewed again, and at that same time, Spotify's official support account tweeted that the service had been restored.
  Unfortunately, outages and problems caused by expired certificates are becoming more common as most services have switched over to secure connections.
 Earlier this month, a certificate expired for California's CalREDIE infectious disease reporting and monitoring system that led to an  underreporting of COVID-19 cases in the state.
 In the past, we have also seen expired certificates affect services such as Facebook's  Tor server,  Microsoft Teams, and  IoT devices.
    Lawrence Abrams is the creator and owner of BleepingComputer.com. Lawrence's area of expertise includes malware removal and computer forensics. Lawrence Abrams is a co-author of the Winternals Defragmentation, Recovery, and Administration Field Guide and the technical editor for Rootkits for Dummies.
title: Spotify在忘记续订证书后遇到停机
title_en: Spotify hit with outage after forgetting to renew a certificate

仇恨可能是一个很强烈的词。他们觉得我觉得他们很烦人。而且很笨。而且很烦人。这与事实大相径庭。
我对他们的感觉和我看起来的行为方式不一致，并不是因为我喜怒无常或脾气暴躁，或者，你知道，我想一个人呆在校园钟楼上。
从2009年到2010年，我拥有一部手机，由于一些不幸的故障，我发送的每一条短信都在末尾加了三个点，使每一条短信看起来都奇怪地被动-咄咄逼人，或者--在某些情况下--出人意料地暗示。
冒犯朋友。令人困惑的潜在日期。我只需按一下按钮就可以发出社会混乱的脉搏。这是一个关于标点符号的力量的故事--三个小圆点改变了我输入的任何东西的意思，让我的朋友们感到困惑，我想知道十多年后，这些小圆点到底造成了什么损害。甚至不知道如果他们改成感叹号会发生什么。
罪魁祸首是三星Slash，这是一款为维珍移动(Virgin Mobile)预付费系列制造的滑块手机。当时，CNet给了它3颗星(满分5星)，称它是一款不错的入门级手机，有几个额外的功能，使其略高于一部普通手机。它可以放在我的手掌上，完全放在我的后口袋里，滑块风格让它感觉像是某种詹姆斯·邦德(James Bond)式的通讯设备。最重要的是，它可以充电，这与我之前破旧的翻盖手机不同。当时的评论，包括我们的评论，都没有提到这些点。
两周前，在与同事们讨论过度使用感叹号时，我回想起了斜杠的记忆。
我决定问问我大学的朋友们，他们是否还记得臭名昭著的三个点。我想，他们肯定不会这么做的。已经10多年了。在经历了十年的婚姻、孩子、研究生、新的工作、新的城市和所有我们自己更重要的个人细节之后，为什么还会有人坚持这样的细节呢？
我的朋友亚伦在描述了这部手机给他整整一年的极度焦虑之后说，这部手机的社交意义在另一个层面上。当我拿着一些记者票准备去见约翰·梅耶尔的后勤工作时，他给我发短信说，你不想让我去吗？
卡西迪是我在校报的首席同谋，他记得发短信谈论潜在的故事创意：每一次(你)以这三个点结束时，我只是以为(你)是被动地、咄咄逼人地告诉我，我说的一切都是愚蠢的。
另一位朋友梅丽莎(Melissa)说，她觉得我总是在传达一些她没有注意到的东西。你总是含沙射影地说话，感觉很不符合你的性格。
即使这么多年过去了，听起来还是有点令人畏惧。我想回到过去，告诉我20岁的自己把账单寄到2020年，然后买一部该死的iPhone。
这就像是我的邪恶双胞胎正在截取我的通讯，兴高采烈地以最令人讨厌的方式造成严重破坏：错误的标点符号。
事实上，很长一段时间以来，技术一直在为我们的沟通方式增加层次。在雷什玛·索贾尼(Reshma Saujani)2019年出版的“勇敢，不完美”(Brave，Not Perfect)一书中，她谈到有多少女性在电子邮件中使用感叹号和表情符号。是!。好的!。我能做到！
我们热情洋溢，都是为了看起来友好和平易近人，绝对不是不讨人喜欢，甚至更不是直接。关于如何在短信末尾加一个句号被视为粗鲁的文章太多了，相当于你的妈妈用你的中间名叫你。2015年，“华盛顿邮报”称这是一种针对朋友的心理战行为。
2017年，研究人员给我们用来传达信息中缺失的面部表情和肢体语言的所有奇怪的拼写、表情符号和标点符号取了一个名字：文本。当你给你的朋友发短信时，或者你决定是用什么，还是在后面多贴10个，所有这些都是文本，它们对于增加更多的意义和上下文非常重要。(编者注：这句话的意思是：你给你的朋友发短信，或者决定是用什么，还是在后面多贴10个)，所有这些都是文本，它们对增加更多的意义和上下文非常重要。(译者注：这句话的意思是：你给你的朋友发短信，或者决定是用什么，还是在后面多贴10个)，所有这些都是文本，它们对增加额外的意义和上下文非常重要。每一个标点符号，或者没有标点符号，都很重要。
在那里，我发出省略号，好像我想烧毁我的社交圈，骑马消失在西方。
用最酷的新闻娱乐你的大脑，从流媒体到超级英雄，从迷因到视频游戏。
一旦我意识到发生了什么，我就试着找出如何修复这些点。互联网上只出现了几篇来自其他有同样问题的人的论坛帖子。在没有其他选择的情况下，我开始在短信上签名，就像你爸爸一样，希望在短信内容和那些点之间加点什么，什么都行。
这也让我的朋友们摸不着头脑。艾米丽说：我想我问过你为什么在短信上签名，当你告诉我是因为那些圆点时。一切都变得更有意义了。
解释就这样开始了。每次我和某人交换号码，比如我暗恋的同学，我都会当面或通过短信提出警告，这是一个痛苦的警告。为了让自己听起来很随意，我语无伦次地说，所以我的手机做了一个奇怪的事情，它在我所有的短信上都钉上了三个点，这很尴尬。所以我在短信上签名，这样听起来就不会让人觉得我是被动攻击性的。然后我祈祷他们会记得。
我的前室友金德尔告诉我，这真的很滑稽，即使我知道为什么会这样。她的丈夫尼克在短信中开玩笑地说：我已经结束了对使用…的人的友谊。&#34；
有没有人因为我的短信问题把我甩了？这已经太久了，还不能确定。
然后在2010年12月，也就是我大四的时候，在报纸上看到的最激烈的突发新闻情况中，我的第一部智能手机收到了邮件。在报社，我疯狂地打开小巧的小黑莓手机，把我的号码转过来，设法建立了我的Twitter和电子邮件账户，然后慢跑回到我的公寓，一路发电子邮件。
就这样，一年的紧张关系结束了。我发的短信就是我想说的。我不再恨我的朋友了。当我问我的朋友们那一年为什么不抛弃我时，他们的普遍回答是，他们比我的手机发生的任何事情都更了解我。
诚然，我仍然对我的短信、电子邮件和松弛信息有低调的偏执。我听起来很讽刺吗？突然？正因为如此，我让自己删除了多余的表情符号和感叹号，我依赖这些表情符号和感叹号，以确保没有人再认为我讨厌它们。
我很少生气，但我也不会在这里做后空翻，回复有关会议时间和文章截止日期的信息。幸运的是，我的手机不会泄露任何额外的含义。
尊重他人，保持文明礼貌，不离题。我们删除违反我们政策的评论，我们鼓励您阅读这些评论。我们可以随时关闭讨论线程。
note_en:
Hate might be a strong word. They thought  I thought they were annoying. And dumb. And bothersome. That couldn't have been further from the truth.
 This discrepancy in how I felt about them and how I seemingly acted wasn't because I was moody or short-tempered or, you know, wanted to be left alone to haunt the campus bell tower.
  From 2009 to 2010, I was the owner of a phone which, by some unfortunate glitch, applied three dots onto the end of every single text I sent, making each dispatch seem strangely passive-aggressive or -- in some cases -- unexpectedly suggestive.
    Offending friends. Confusing potential dates. I could send a pulse of social chaos with the press of a button. This is a story about the power of punctuation -- three little dots that morphed the meaning of whatever I was typing, leaving my friends baffled and me wondering more than a decade later what exactly the damage was from those dots. Or even what would have happened if they'd been exclamation marks instead.
  At fault: the  Samsung Slash, a slider phone made for  Virgin Mobile's prepaid range. At the time,  CNET gave it 3 out of 5 stars, calling it "a decent entry-level phone with a few extra features that put it just above a basic handset." It fit in the palm of my hand, and entirely in my back pocket, and the slider style made it feel like some James Bond-esque communication device. Most importantly, it held a charge, unlike my previous worn-out flip phone. None of the reviews from the time, including ours, mentioned the dots.
   Two weeks ago, memories of the Slash came back to me during a Slack conversation with colleagues about the overuse of exclamation marks.
 I decided to ask my friends from college if they remembered the infamous three dots. Surely, they wouldn't, I thought. It's been more than 10 years. Why would anyone hang onto such a detail after a decade of marriages, babies, graduate degrees, new jobs, new cities and all our own more important personal details?
  "The social implications of that phone were on another level," my friend Aaron says, after describing "the extreme anxiety" it gave him for a full year. At one point while working out logistics to go see John Mayer with some press tickets I had, he'd texted me, "Do you not want me to go?"
 Cassidy, my chief co-conspirator on the campus newspaper, remembers texting about potential story ideas: "Every single time [you] ended with these three dots, I just assumed [you were] passive aggressively telling me that everything I said was dumb."
  Another friend, Melissa, says she felt like I was always communicating something she just wasn't picking up on. "It felt very out of character for you to be constantly speaking in innuendo."
 Even all these years later, it's a bit cringey to hear. I want to reach back into the past and tell my 20-year-old self to forward the bills to 2020 and buy a damn iPhone.
 It was like my evil twin was intercepting my communications, gleefully causing havoc in the nerdiest way possible: errant punctuation.
  In reality, technology has been adding layers to the way we communicate for a long time. In  Reshma Saujani's 2019 book Brave, Not Perfect, she talks about how many women litter their emails with exclamation marks and  emoji. Yes! Sure! Can do!
 We are deliriously enthusiastic, all in an effort to seem friendly and approachable and definitely not unlikable, or even worse -- direct. There is a surfeit of articles about how adding a period to the end of texts is seen as curt, the equivalent of your mom calling you by your middle name. The Washington Post, in 2015, called it " an act of psychological warfare against your friends."
 In 2017, researchers gave a name to all the weird spellings, emoji and punctuation we use to convey the facial expressions and body language absent from messaging:  textisms. When you message your friend "!!!!!!" or decide between using "wut" and "what?" or stick about 10 extra s's on the end of a "yes," all of those are textisms, and they're wildly important in adding additional meaning and context. Every punctuation mark, or lack thereof, matters.
 And there I was sending out ellipsis dots like I wanted to burn down my social circle and disappear into the West on horseback.
 Entertain your brain with the coolest news from streaming to superheroes, memes to video games.
 As soon I realized what was happening, I tried to figure out how to fix the dots. The internet turned up only a few forum posts from other people with the same issue. With no other options, I started signing my texts "-- EC," like your dad does, in hopes of putting something, anything, in between the content of my texts and those dots.
  That too, was head-scratching to my friends. "I think I asked you why you signed your texts and that's when you told me it was because of the dots," Emily says. "Everything made more sense."
 And so began the explanations. Every time I exchanged numbers with someone, like a classmate I had a crush on, I offered either in person or via text, a painfully crafted warning. Trying to sound casual, I rattled off, "So my phone does this weird thing where it tacks three dots onto all my texts, which is pretty awkward. So I sign my texts so it doesn't sound like I'm being passive-aggressive." And then I prayed they would remember.
 "It was really hilarious, even when I knew why it was happening," my former roommate Kindall tells me. Her husband, Nick, offers jokingly on the text thread: "I have ended friendships over people using …"
 Had anyone dropped  me because of my texting issues? It's been too long to know for sure.
 And then in December 2010, my senior year, in the middle of the most intense breaking-news situation the newspaper had ever seen, my first smartphone arrived in the mail. In the newspaper office, I frantically unwrapped the compact little  BlackBerry and ported over my number, managing to set up my Twitter and email accounts before jogging back to my apartment, emailing all the way.
 And just like that, a year of strained relationships ended. Whatever I texted was exactly what I meant. I didn't hate my friends anymore. And when I asked my friends why they didn't ditch me that year, the general answer was that they knew me better than whatever was happening with my phone.
 Admittedly, I still have low-lying paranoia about my texts, emails and Slack messages. Do I sound sarcastic? Abrupt? So much so, I make myself delete the excess emoji and exclamation marks I've come to rely on to make sure no one ever thinks I hate them again.
 I'm rarely mad, but I'm also not doing backflips over here, answering messages about meeting times and article deadlines. Mercifully, my phone won't betray any additional meaning.

title_en: The phone that almost ruined my social life
